perturbation.original_text: At any time that there is no uncompleted Statement of Work outstanding, either party may terminate this Agreement for any or no reason upon fifteen (15) days advance notice to the other.
perturbation.changed_text: At any time that there is no uncompleted Statement of Work outstanding, either party may terminate this Agreement for any or no reason upon five (5) days advance notice to the other.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period from 15 days to 5 days creates a potential conflict with standard contractual principles requiring reasonable notice, and it could violate implied duties of good faith and fair dealing, especially if such short notice causes significant harm.
perturbation.law_explanation: Although, Arizona is an at-will employment state and does not prevent termination of an employment contract with only 5 days notice. It may not be reasonable if one party relies on future income to conduct business, therefore 5 days is not enough time to find a replacement job. Thus this can contradict the law.

ARS Title Page Previous Section Title 23 Next Section Printable Version 23-1501 - Severability of employment relationships; protection from retaliatory discharges; exclusivity of statutory remedies in employment 23-1501 . Severability of employment relationships; protection from retaliatory discharges; exclusivity of statutory remedies in employment A. The public policy of this state is that: 1. The employment relationship is contractual in nature. 2. The employment relationship is severable at the pleasure of either the employee or the employer unless both the employee and the employer have signed a written contract to the contrary setting forth that the employment relationship shall remain in effect for a specified duration of time or otherwise expressly restricting the right of either party to terminate the employment relationship. Both the employee and the employer must sign this written contract, or this written contract must be set forth in the employment handbook or manual or any similar document distributed to the employee, if that document expresses the intent that it is a contract of employment, or this written contract must be set forth in a writing signed by the party to be charged. Partial performance of employment shall not be deemed sufficient to eliminate the requirements set forth in this paragraph. Nothing in this paragraph shall be construed to affect the rights of public employees under the Constitution of Arizona and state and local laws of this state or the rights of employees and employers as defined by a collective bargaining agreement. 3. An employee has a claim against an employer for termination of employment only if one or more of the following circumstances have occurred: (a) The employer has terminated the employment relationship of an employee in breach of an employment contract, as set forth in paragraph 2 of this subsection, in which case the remedies for the breach are limited to the remedies for a breach of contract. (b) The employer has terminated the employment relationship of an employee in violation of a
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the termination notice period from 15 days to 5 days, which the Perturbation Explanation identifies as potentially conflicting with contractual principles requiring reasonable notice and good faith. The Law Explanation further suggests that 5 days' notice might be unreasonable, particularly if it causes significant harm due to reliance on future income. Scraped Law Snippet 1, A.R.S. 23-1501, establishes that employment relationships are contractual and severable at will unless a written contract specifies otherwise. While it doesn't mandate a specific notice period, the reduction to 5 days could be challenged as unreasonable, potentially contradicting the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing inherent in contractual relationships, making the altered contract terms potentially unenforceable. Because the change could be seen as undermining the implicit requirement of reasonable notice, this qualifies as a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Neither party shall, during the term of this Agreement and for one (1) year after its termination, solicit for hire as an employee, consultant or otherwise any of the other party's personnel who have had direct involvement with the Services, without such other party's express written consent, which shall not be unreasonably withheld.
perturbation.changed_text: Neither party shall, during the term of this Agreement and for two (2) years after its termination, solicit for hire as an employee, consultant or otherwise any of the other party's personnel who have had direct involvement with the Services, without such other party's express written consent, which shall not be unreasonably withheld.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-solicitation period to two years may be deemed unreasonably long, acting as an undue restraint on trade or an individual's ability to pursue employment, therefore violating state laws concerning restraint of trade.
perturbation.law_explanation: Arizona Revised Statutes § 23-1501 addresses agreements in restraint of trade. While non-solicitation agreements are generally enforceable, a two-year restriction, particularly without specific geographic or industry limitations, might be seen as an unreasonable restraint on an employee's ability to find work and an employer's ability to hire, therefore violating the statute's implied limitations against overly broad restrictions.

perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-solicitation period to two years, which the Law Explanation indicates might be seen as an unreasonable restraint on an employee's ability to find work. The Perturbation Explanation supports this, stating that a longer period may be an undue restraint on trade. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Arizona Revised Statutes § 23-1501, addresses agreements in restraint of trade. While it doesn't explicitly define what constitutes an unreasonable restraint, the extension introduces a potential conflict with this statute by possibly creating an overly broad restriction.
